title: Messenger RNA

infobox.value: (**mRNA**) is a single-stranded [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") of [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") that corresponds to the [genetic sequence](/wiki/Genetic_sequence "Genetic sequence") of a [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene"), and is read by a [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") in the process of [synthesizing](/wiki/Protein_biosynthesis "Protein biosynthesis") a [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein").[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

RNA that is read by the ribosome to produce a protein

"mRNA" redirects here. For other uses, see [mRNA (disambiguation)](/wiki/MRNA_(disambiguation) "MRNA (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [modRNA](/wiki/ModRNA "ModRNA") or [mDNA](/wiki/MDNA "MDNA").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/MRNA-interaction.svg/330px-MRNA-interaction.svg.png)](/wiki/File:MRNA-interaction.svg)

The "life cycle" of an mRNA in a [eukaryotic](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") cell. [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") is [transcribed](/wiki/Transcription_(genetics) "Transcription (genetics)") in the [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"); after [processing](/wiki/Post-transcriptional_modification "Post-transcriptional modification"), it is transported to the [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm") and [translated](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") by the [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome"). Finally, the mRNA is degraded.

**Messenger ribonucleic acid** (**mRNA**) is a single-stranded [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") of [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") that corresponds to the [genetic sequence](/wiki/Genetic_sequence "Genetic sequence") of a [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene"), and is read by a [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") in the process of [synthesizing](/wiki/Protein_biosynthesis "Protein biosynthesis") a [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein").[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

mRNA is created during the process of [transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(biology) "Transcription (biology)"), where an [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") ([RNA polymerase](/wiki/RNA_polymerase "RNA polymerase")) converts the gene into [primary transcript](/wiki/Primary_transcript "Primary transcript") mRNA (also known as [pre-mRNA](/wiki/Pre-mRNA "Pre-mRNA")).[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_ProkTranscription-4) This pre-mRNA usually still contains [introns](/wiki/Introns "Introns"), regions that will not go on to code for the final [amino acid sequence](/wiki/Amino_acid_sequence "Amino acid sequence"). These are removed in the process of [RNA splicing](/wiki/RNA_splicing "RNA splicing"), leaving only [exons](/wiki/Exons "Exons"), regions that will encode the protein.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6) This exon sequence constitutes [mature mRNA](/wiki/Mature_mRNA "Mature mRNA"). Mature mRNA is then read by the ribosome, and the ribosome creates the protein utilizing [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acids "Amino acids") carried by [transfer RNA](/wiki/Transfer_RNA "Transfer RNA") (tRNA). This process is known as [translation](/wiki/Translation_(biology) "Translation (biology)").[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1) All of these processes form part of the [central dogma of molecular biology](/wiki/Central_dogma_of_molecular_biology "Central dogma of molecular biology"), which describes the flow of genetic information in a biological system.[[7]](#cite_note-Crick1970CentralDogma-7)

As in [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA"), genetic information in mRNA is contained in the sequence of [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotides "Nucleotides"), which are arranged into [codons](/wiki/Codons "Codons") consisting of three [ribonucleotides](/wiki/Ribonucleotide "Ribonucleotide") each. Each codon codes for a specific [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid"), except the [stop codons](/wiki/Stop_codon "Stop codon"), which terminate protein synthesis.[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1) The translation of codons into amino acids requires two other types of RNA: transfer RNA, which recognizes the codon and provides the corresponding amino acid, and [ribosomal RNA](/wiki/Ribosomal_RNA "Ribosomal RNA") (rRNA), the central component of the ribosome's protein-manufacturing machinery.[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1)

The concept of mRNA was first conceived by [Sydney Brenner](/wiki/Sydney_Brenner "Sydney Brenner") and [Francis Crick](/wiki/Francis_Crick "Francis Crick") in 1960 during a conversation with [François Jacob](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Jacob "François Jacob").[[8]](#cite_note-Cobb-8) In May 1961, messenger RNA was experimentally characterized in two back-to-back *Nature* papers: one by Brenner, Jacob, and Meselson, and one by Gros and colleagues (including Watson).[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) While analyzing the data in preparation for publication, Jacob and Jacques Monod coined the term "messenger RNA".[[11]](#cite_note-DNALC_WhoNamedmRNA-11)

## Synthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Synthesis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/DNA_transcription.svg/250px-DNA_transcription.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DNA_transcription.svg)

RNA polymerase transcribes a DNA strand to form mRNA

The brief existence of an mRNA molecule begins with transcription, and ultimately ends in degradation. During its life, an mRNA molecule may also be processed, edited, and transported prior to translation.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6) Eukaryotic mRNA molecules often require extensive processing and transport, while [prokaryotic](/wiki/Prokaryotes "Prokaryotes") mRNA molecules do not.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[12]](#cite_note-Irastortza2021_CTT-12) A molecule of [eukaryotic](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") mRNA and the proteins surrounding it are together called a [messenger RNP](/wiki/Messenger_RNP "Messenger RNP")[[13]](#cite_note-13).

### Transcription

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Transcription")]

Main article: [Transcription (genetics)](/wiki/Transcription_(genetics) "Transcription (genetics)")

Transcription is the process by which genetic information stored in DNA is copied into RNA by the enzyme [RNA polymerase](/wiki/RNA_polymerase "RNA polymerase").[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3) During transcription, RNA polymerase binds to a promoter sequence on the DNA and synthesizes a complementary RNA strand (mRNA) from the DNA template.[[4]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_ProkTranscription-4)[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3)

This process differs between prokaryotes and eukaryotes. In prokaryotes, transcription occurs in the cytoplasm.[[4]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_ProkTranscription-4) Because prokaryotes lack a membrane-bound nucleus, ribosomes can attach to the nascent mRNA strand and begin translation while transcription is still in progress.[[12]](#cite_note-Irastortza2021_CTT-12)

In eukaryotes, transcription occurs within the cell nucleus.[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6) The initial product of transcription is not functional mRNA but is termed *precursor mRNA* or *[pre-mRNA](/wiki/Pre-mRNA "Pre-mRNA")*.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) This pre-mRNA must undergo extensive processing (including 5' capping, splicing to remove non-coding introns, and 3' polyadenylation) to become *[mature mRNA](/wiki/Mature_mRNA "Mature mRNA")*.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6) Once processed, the mature mRNA is exported from the nucleus to the cytoplasm for translation.[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6)

### Uracil substitution for thymine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Uracil substitution for thymine")]

Whereas DNA contains thymine (T), RNA contains uracil (U).[[14]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_MolecularComposition-14) During the process of transcription, the enzyme RNA polymerase incorporates uracil opposite adenine bases located on the DNA template strand. Therefore, the resulting RNA transcript contains uracil in the positions where the coding DNA strand contains thymine.[[14]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_MolecularComposition-14)[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3)

Structurally, uracil–adenine (U–A) base pairs closely resemble thymine–adenine (T–A) base pairs, which ensures that the genetic information carried by the sequence is faithfully preserved.[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3)

A frequently cited explanation for the presence of thymine in DNA involves the necessity of genome maintenance. Because cytosine can spontaneously deaminate to form uracil, DNA repair systems recognize uracil as a form of damage. The utilization of thymine as a standard base allows the cell to distinguish legitimate bases from errors, thereby maintaining uracil as a specific signal for repair.[[15]](#cite_note-Vertessy2009_KeepingUracilOut-15)

### Eukaryotic pre-mRNA processing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Eukaryotic pre-mRNA processing")]

Main article: [Post-transcriptional modification](/wiki/Post-transcriptional_modification "Post-transcriptional modification")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Gene_structure_eukaryote_2_annotated.svg/250px-Gene_structure_eukaryote_2_annotated.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gene_structure_eukaryote_2_annotated.svg)

DNA gene is transcribed to pre-mRNA, which is then processed to form a mature mRNA, and then lastly translated by a ribosome to a protein

Processing of mRNA differs greatly among [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria"), and [archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea").[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3) Non-eukaryotic mRNA is, in essence, mature upon transcription and requires no processing, except in rare cases.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Eukaryotic pre-mRNA, however, requires several processing steps before its transport to the cytoplasm and its translation by the ribosome.

#### Splicing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Splicing")]

Main article: [RNA splicing](/wiki/RNA_splicing "RNA splicing")

The extensive processing of eukaryotic pre-mRNA that leads to the mature mRNA is the [RNA splicing](/wiki/RNA_splicing "RNA splicing"), a mechanism by which [introns](/wiki/Intron "Intron") or [outrons](/wiki/Outron "Outron") (non-coding regions) are removed and [exons](/wiki/Exon "Exon") (coding regions) are joined.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### 5' cap addition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 5' cap addition")]

Main article: [5' cap](/wiki/5%27_cap "5' cap")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/5%27_cap_labeled.svg/250px-5%27_cap_labeled.svg.png)](/wiki/File:5%27_cap_labeled.svg)

5' cap structure

A *5' cap* (also termed an RNA cap, an RNA [7-methylguanosine](/wiki/7-methylguanosine "7-methylguanosine") cap, or an RNA m7G cap) is a modified guanine nucleotide that has been added to the "front" or [5' end](/wiki/5%27_end "5' end") of a eukaryotic messenger RNA shortly after the start of transcription. The 5' cap consists of a terminal 7-methylguanosine residue that is linked through a 5'-5'-triphosphate bond to the first transcribed nucleotide. Its presence is critical for recognition by the [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") and protection from [RNases](/wiki/RNase "RNase").[[19]](#cite_note-Ramanathan2016-19)

Cap addition is coupled to transcription, and occurs co-transcriptionally, such that each influences the other. Shortly after the start of transcription, the 5' end of the mRNA being synthesized is bound by a [cap-synthesizing complex](/wiki/Capping_enzyme "Capping enzyme") associated with [RNA polymerase](/wiki/RNA_polymerase "RNA polymerase"). This [enzymatic](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") complex [catalyzes](/wiki/Catalyze "Catalyze") the chemical reactions that are required for mRNA capping. Synthesis proceeds as a multi-step [biochemical](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry") reaction.[[20]](#cite_note-HsinManley2012-20)

#### Editing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Editing")]

In some instances, an mRNA molecule is [edited](/wiki/RNA_editing "RNA editing"), which changes the nucleotide composition of the transcript. A prominent example in humans involves the [apolipoprotein B](/wiki/Apolipoprotein_B#RNA_editing "Apolipoprotein B") mRNA. In certain tissues, RNA editing of this transcript creates a premature stop codon, which results in the production of a shorter protein variant. Another well studied mechanism is A-to-I (adenosine-to-inosine) editing. This reaction is catalyzed by ADAR enzymes (adenosine deaminase acting on RNA) and typically occurs within double-stranded RNA regions. A-to-I editing may occur in both coding sequences and untranslated regions. Through these modifications, the process can affect protein recoding, RNA structure, and gene regulation.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Polyadenylation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Polyadenylation")]

Main article: [Polyadenylation](/wiki/Polyadenylation "Polyadenylation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Polyadenylation.png/250px-Polyadenylation.png)](/wiki/File:Polyadenylation.png)

Polyadenylation

Polyadenylation is the covalent linkage of a polyadenylyl moiety to a messenger RNA molecule. In eukaryotic organisms most messenger RNA (mRNA) molecules are polyadenylated at the 3' end, but recent studies have shown that short stretches of uridine (oligouridylation) are also common.[[22]](#cite_note-Choi_2012-22) The [poly(A) tail](#Poly(A)_tail) and the protein bound to it aid in protecting mRNA from degradation by exonucleases. Polyadenylation is also important for transcription termination, export of the mRNA from the nucleus, and translation.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6) mRNA can also be polyadenylated in prokaryotic organisms, where poly(A) tails act to facilitate, rather than impede, exonucleolytic degradation.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Polyadenylation occurs during and/or immediately after transcription of DNA into RNA.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) After transcription has been terminated, the mRNA chain is cleaved through the action of an endonuclease complex associated with RNA polymerase. After the mRNA has been cleaved, around 200–250 adenosine residues are added to the free 3' end at the cleavage site. This reaction is catalyzed by [polyadenylate polymerase](/wiki/Polyadenylate_polymerase "Polyadenylate polymerase").[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[3]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromDNAtoRNA-3) Just as in [alternative splicing](/wiki/Alternative_splicing "Alternative splicing"), there can be more than one polyadenylation variant of an mRNA.

Polyadenylation site mutations can occur. The primary RNA transcript of a gene is cleaved at the poly(A) addition site, and about 150–250 adenosines are added to the 3′ end of the RNA as a poly(A) tail.[[24]](#cite_note-Curinha2014-24) If this site is altered, cleavage and polyadenylation can shift to a downstream poly(A) site, producing an abnormally long and unstable mRNA.[[25]](#cite_note-DiGiammartino2011-25)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transport")]

Another difference between eukaryotes and prokaryotes is mRNA transport. Because eukaryotic transcription and translation is compartmentally separated, eukaryotic mRNAs must be exported from the [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus") to the [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm")—a process that may be regulated by different signaling pathways.[[26]](#cite_note-Quaresma2013-26) Mature mRNAs are recognized by their processed modifications and then exported through the [nuclear pore](/wiki/Nuclear_pore "Nuclear pore") by binding to the cap-binding proteins CBP20 and CBP80,[[27]](#cite_note-kierzkowski2009-27) as well as the transcription/export complex (TREX).[[28]](#cite_note-strausser2002-28)[[29]](#cite_note-katahira2014-29) Multiple mRNA export pathways have been identified in eukaryotes.[[30]](#cite_note-Cenik2011-30)

In spatially complex cells, some mRNAs are transported to particular subcellular destinations. In mature [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron"), certain mRNA are transported from the [soma](/wiki/Soma_(biology) "Soma (biology)") to [dendrites](/wiki/Dendrite "Dendrite"). One site of mRNA translation is at polyribosomes selectively localized beneath synapses.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The mRNA for [Arc/Arg3.1](/wiki/Arc/Arg3.1 "Arc/Arg3.1") is induced by synaptic activity and localizes selectively near active [synapses](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") based on signals generated by [NMDA receptors](/wiki/NMDA_receptor "NMDA receptor").[[32]](#cite_note-32) Other mRNAs also move into dendrites in response to external stimuli, such as [β-actin](/wiki/%CE%92-actin "Β-actin") mRNA.[[33]](#cite_note-Job1912-33) For export from the nucleus, actin mRNA associates with [ZBP1](/wiki/ZBP1 "ZBP1")[[34]](#cite_note-Oleynikov2003-34) and later with [40S subunit](/wiki/Eukaryotic_small_ribosomal_subunit_(40S) "Eukaryotic small ribosomal subunit (40S)"). The complex is bound by a [motor protein](/wiki/Motor_protein "Motor protein") and is transported to the target location ([neurite extension](/wiki/Neurite "Neurite")) along the [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton"). Eventually ZBP1 is [phosphorylated](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") by [Src](/wiki/Src_family_kinase "Src family kinase") in order for translation to be initiated.[[35]](#cite_note-Hüttelmaier_2005-35) In developing neurons, mRNAs are also transported into growing [axons](/wiki/Axon "Axon") and especially growth cones. Many mRNAs are marked with so-called "zip codes", which target their transport to a specific location.[[36]](#cite_note-Oleynikov1998-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Ainger1997-37) mRNAs can also transfer between mammalian cells through structures called [tunneling nanotubes](/wiki/Tunneling_nanotube "Tunneling nanotube").[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Translation")]

Main article: [Translation (biology)](/wiki/Translation_(biology) "Translation (biology)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Peptide_syn.svg/250px-Peptide_syn.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Peptide_syn.svg)

Translation of mRNA to protein

Because prokaryotic mRNA does not need to be processed or transported, translation by the [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") can begin immediately after the end of transcription. Therefore, it can be said that prokaryotic translation is *coupled* to transcription and occurs *co-transcriptionally*.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In [eukaryotic cells](/wiki/Eukaryotic_cell "Eukaryotic cell") the process of [translation](/wiki/Translation_(biology) "Translation (biology)") starts with the information stored in the nucleotide sequence of [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA"). This is first transformed into mRNA, then [transfer RNA](/wiki/Transfer_RNA "Transfer RNA") (tRNA) specifies which three-nucleotide codon from the genetic code corresponds to which amino acid.[[41]](#cite_note-crick-41)

Eukaryotic mRNA that has been processed and transported to the cytoplasm (i.e., mature mRNA) can then be translated by ribosomes. Translation may occur at ribosomes free in the cytoplasm, or targeted to the [endoplasmic reticulum](/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum "Endoplasmic reticulum") by the [signal recognition particle](/wiki/Signal_recognition_particle "Signal recognition particle"). Therefore, unlike in prokaryotes, eukaryotic translation is not directly coupled to transcription. In some contexts, protein abundance can increase even when mRNA abundance decreases, because translation efficiency and protein turnover are regulated independently of transcript levels; this has been reported for mRNA and protein levels of [EEF1A1](/wiki/Eukaryotic_translation_elongation_factor_1_alpha_1 "Eukaryotic translation elongation factor 1 alpha 1") in [breast cancer](/wiki/Breast_cancer "Breast cancer").[[42]](#cite_note-Lin2018-42)[[43]](#cite_note-VogelMarcotte2012-43)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/MRNA_structure.svg/960px-MRNA_structure.svg.png)](/wiki/File:MRNA_structure.svg)

The structure of a mature eukaryotic mRNA. A fully processed mRNA includes a [5' cap](/wiki/5%27_cap "5' cap"), [5' UTR](/wiki/5%27_UTR "5' UTR"), [coding region](/wiki/Coding_region "Coding region"), [3' UTR](/wiki/3%27_UTR "3' UTR"), and poly(A) tail.

### Coding regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Coding regions")]

Main article: [Coding region](/wiki/Coding_region "Coding region")

Coding regions are composed of [codons](/wiki/Codons "Codons"), which are decoded and translated into proteins by the ribosome; in eukaryotes usually into one and in prokaryotes usually into several. Coding regions begin with the [start codon](/wiki/Start_codon "Start codon") and end with a [stop codon](/wiki/Stop_codon "Stop codon"). In general, the start codon is an AUG triplet and the stop codon is UAG ("amber"), UAA ("ochre"), or UGA ("opal").[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1) The coding regions tend to be stabilized by internal base pairs; this impedes degradation.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) In addition to being protein-coding, portions of coding regions may serve as regulatory sequences in the [pre-mRNA](/wiki/Pre-mRNA "Pre-mRNA") as [exonic splicing enhancers](/wiki/Exonic_splicing_enhancer "Exonic splicing enhancer") or [exonic splicing silencers](/wiki/Exonic_splicing_silencer "Exonic splicing silencer").

### Untranslated regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Untranslated regions")]

Main articles: [5' UTR](/wiki/5%27_UTR "5' UTR") and [3' UTR](/wiki/3%27_UTR "3' UTR")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Fbioe-09-718753-g002.jpg/250px-Fbioe-09-718753-g002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fbioe-09-718753-g002.jpg)

Universal structure of eukaryotic mRNA, showing the structure of the 5' and 3' UTRs.

Untranslated regions (UTRs) are sections of the mRNA before the start codon and after the stop codon that are not translated, termed the [five prime untranslated region](/wiki/Five_prime_untranslated_region "Five prime untranslated region") (5' UTR) and [three prime untranslated region](/wiki/Three_prime_untranslated_region "Three prime untranslated region") (3' UTR), respectively.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) These regions are transcribed with the coding region and thus are [exonic](/wiki/Exon "Exon") as they are present in the mature mRNA.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) Several roles in gene expression have been attributed to the untranslated regions, including mRNA stability, mRNA localization, and [translational efficiency](/wiki/Translational_efficiency "Translational efficiency"). The ability of a UTR to perform these functions depends on the sequence of the UTR and can differ between mRNAs.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) Genetic variants in 3' UTR have also been implicated in disease susceptibility because of the change in RNA structure and protein translation.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The stability of mRNAs may be controlled by the 5' UTR and/or 3' UTR due to varying affinity for RNA degrading enzymes called [ribonucleases](/wiki/Ribonuclease "Ribonuclease") and for ancillary proteins that can promote or inhibit RNA degradation.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) (See also, [C-rich stability element](/wiki/C-rich_stability_element "C-rich stability element").)

Translational efficiency, including sometimes the complete inhibition of translation, can be controlled by UTRs.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) Proteins that bind to either the 3' or 5' UTR may affect translation by influencing the ribosome's ability to bind to the mRNA.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) [MicroRNAs](/wiki/MicroRNA "MicroRNA") bound to the [3' UTR](/wiki/3%27_UTR "3' UTR") also may affect translational efficiency or mRNA stability.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Eulalio2009-49)

Cytoplasmic localization of mRNA is thought to be a function of the 3' UTR.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) Proteins that are needed in a particular region of the cell can also be translated there; in such a case, the 3' UTR may contain sequences that allow the transcript to be localized to this region for translation.[[33]](#cite_note-Job1912-33)

Some of the elements contained in untranslated regions form a characteristic [secondary structure](/wiki/Secondary_structure "Secondary structure") when transcribed into RNA. These structural mRNA elements are involved in regulating the mRNA.[[46]](#cite_note-Mignone2002UTR-46) Some, such as the [SECIS element](/wiki/SECIS_element "SECIS element"), are targets for proteins to bind.[[50]](#cite_note-Liu1998SECIS-50) One class of mRNA element, the [riboswitches](/wiki/Riboswitch "Riboswitch"), directly bind small molecules, changing their fold to modify levels of transcription or translation. In these cases, the mRNA regulates itself.[[51]](#cite_note-SerganovNudler2013-51)

### Poly(A) tail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Poly(A) tail")]

Main article: [Polyadenylation](/wiki/Polyadenylation "Polyadenylation")

The 3' poly(A) tail is a long sequence of [adenine](/wiki/Adenine "Adenine") nucleotides (often several hundred) added to the [3' end](/wiki/3%27_end "3' end") of the pre-mRNA. This tail promotes export from the nucleus and translation, and protects the mRNA from degradation.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Hocine2010_RNAprocessingExport-6)

### Monocistronic versus polycistronic mRNA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Monocistronic versus polycistronic mRNA")]

See also: [Cistron](/wiki/Cistron "Cistron")

An mRNA molecule is said to be monocistronic when it contains the genetic information to [translate](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") only a single [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") chain (polypeptide). This is the case for most of the [eukaryotic](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") mRNAs.[[52]](#cite_note-Kozak_1983-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) On the other hand, polycistronic mRNA carries several [open reading frames](/wiki/Open_reading_frame "Open reading frame") (ORFs), each of which is translated into a polypeptide. These polypeptides usually have a related function (they often are the subunits composing a final complex protein) and their coding sequence is grouped and regulated together in a regulatory region, containing a [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(biology) "Promoter (biology)") and an [operator](/wiki/Operator_(biology) "Operator (biology)"). Most of the mRNA found in [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") and [archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea") is polycistronic,[[52]](#cite_note-Kozak_1983-52) as is the human mitochondrial genome.[[54]](#cite_note-MercerNeph2011-54) Dicistronic or bicistronic mRNA encodes only two [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein").

### mRNA circularization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: mRNA circularization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Fgene-10-00006-g001.jpg/250px-Fgene-10-00006-g001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fgene-10-00006-g001.jpg)

mRNA circularization and regulation

In eukaryotes mRNA molecules form circular structures due to an interaction between the [eIF4E](/wiki/EIF4E "EIF4E") and [poly(A)-binding protein](/wiki/Poly(A)-binding_protein "Poly(A)-binding protein"), which both bind to [eIF4G](/wiki/EIF4G "EIF4G"), forming an mRNA-protein-mRNA bridge.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Circularization is thought to promote cycling of ribosomes on the mRNA leading to time-efficient translation, and may also function to ensure only intact mRNA are translated (partially degraded mRNA characteristically have no m7G cap, or no poly-A tail).[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Other mechanisms for circularization exist, particularly in viruses. In several RNA viruses, long-distance RNA interactions and/or protein-mediated bridges between the 5' and 3' ends can promote translation and genome replication.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

RNA virus genomes (the + strands of which are translated as mRNA) are also commonly circularized.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## Degradation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Degradation")]

Different mRNAs within the same cell have distinct lifetimes (stabilities). In bacterial cells, individual mRNAs can survive from seconds to more than an hour. However, the lifetime averages between 1 and 3 minutes, making bacterial mRNA much less stable than eukaryotic mRNA.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In mammalian cells, mRNA lifetimes range from several minutes to days.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The greater the stability of an mRNA the more protein may be produced from that mRNA.[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1) The limited lifetime of mRNA enables a cell to alter protein synthesis rapidly in response to its changing needs.[[1]](#cite_note-Alberts2002_FromRNAtoProtein-1) There are many mechanisms that lead to the destruction of an mRNA, some of which are described below.

### Prokaryotic mRNA degradation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Prokaryotic mRNA degradation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/1-s2.0-S1874939913000436-gr1_lrg.jpg/250px-1-s2.0-S1874939913000436-gr1_lrg.jpg)](/wiki/File:1-s2.0-S1874939913000436-gr1_lrg.jpg)

Overview of mRNA decay pathways in the different life domains.

In general, in prokaryotes the lifetime of mRNA is much shorter than in eukaryotes. Prokaryotes degrade messages by using a combination of ribonucleases, including [endonucleases](/wiki/Endonuclease "Endonuclease"), 3' [exonucleases](/wiki/Exonuclease "Exonuclease"), and 5' exonucleases.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) In some instances, [small RNA molecules](/wiki/Small_RNA "Small RNA") (sRNA) tens to hundreds of nucleotides long can stimulate the degradation of specific mRNAs by base-pairing with complementary sequences and facilitating ribonuclease cleavage by [RNase III](/wiki/RNase_III "RNase III").[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) It was recently shown that bacteria also have a sort of [5' cap](/wiki/5%27_cap "5' cap") consisting of a triphosphate on the [5' end](/wiki/5%27_end "5' end").[[61]](#cite_note-Deana2008-61) Removal of two of the phosphates leaves a 5' monophosphate, causing the message to be destroyed by the exonuclease RNase J, which degrades 5' to 3'.

### Eukaryotic mRNA turnover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Eukaryotic mRNA turnover")]

Inside eukaryotic cells, there is a balance between the processes of [translation](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") and mRNA decay. Messages that are being actively translated are typically associated with cap-binding and translation initiation factors and with [poly(A)-binding protein](/wiki/Poly(A)-binding_protein "Poly(A)-binding protein"), which can antagonize deadenylation and decapping and help protect mRNA ends from decay machinery.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) The balance between translation and decay is reflected in the size and abundance of cytoplasmic structures known as [P-bodies](/wiki/P-bodies "P-bodies").[[62]](#cite_note-Parker2007-62) The [poly(A) tail](/wiki/Polyadenylation "Polyadenylation") of the mRNA is shortened by specialized exonucleases that are targeted to specific messenger RNAs by a combination of cis-regulatory sequences on the RNA and trans-acting RNA-binding proteins.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) Poly(A) tail removal is thought to disrupt the circular structure of the message and destabilize the [cap binding complex](/wiki/Cap_binding_complex "Cap binding complex"). The message is then subject to degradation by either the [exosome complex](/wiki/Exosome_complex "Exosome complex") or the [decapping complex](/wiki/Decapping_complex "Decapping complex").[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) In this way, translationally inactive messages can be destroyed quickly, while active messages remain intact. The mechanism by which translation stops and the message is handed-off to decay complexes is not understood in detail.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5) The majority of mRNA decay was believed to be cytoplasmic; however, recently, a novel mRNA decay pathway was described, which starts in the nucleus.[[63]](#cite_note-Chattopadhyay2022-63)

### AU-rich element decay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: AU-rich element decay")]

The presence of [AU-rich elements](/wiki/AU-rich_element "AU-rich element") in some mammalian mRNAs tends to destabilize those transcripts through the action of cellular proteins that bind these sequences and stimulate [poly(A)](/wiki/Poly(A) "Poly(A)") tail removal.[[64]](#cite_note-Chen1995-64)[[65]](#cite_note-Chen2001-65) Loss of the poly(A) tail is thought to promote mRNA degradation by facilitating attack by both the [exosome complex](/wiki/Exosome_complex "Exosome complex")[[65]](#cite_note-Chen2001-65) and the [decapping complex](/wiki/Decapping_complex "Decapping complex").[[66]](#cite_note-66) Rapid mRNA degradation via [AU-rich elements](/wiki/AU-rich_element "AU-rich element") is a critical mechanism for preventing the overproduction of potent cytokines such as tumor necrosis factor (TNF) and granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor (GM-CSF).[[67]](#cite_note-Shaw1986-67) AU-rich elements also regulate the biosynthesis of proto-oncogenic transcription factors like [c-Jun](/wiki/C-Jun "C-Jun") and [c-Fos](/wiki/C-Fos "C-Fos").[[64]](#cite_note-Chen1995-64)

### Nonsense-mediated decay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Nonsense-mediated decay")]

Main article: [Nonsense-mediated decay](/wiki/Nonsense-mediated_decay "Nonsense-mediated decay")

Eukaryotic messages are subject to surveillance by [nonsense-mediated decay](/wiki/Nonsense-mediated_decay "Nonsense-mediated decay") (NMD), which checks for the presence of premature stop codons (nonsense codons) in the message. These can arise via incomplete splicing, [V(D)J recombination](/wiki/V(D)J_recombination "V(D)J recombination") in the [adaptive immune system](/wiki/Adaptive_immune_system "Adaptive immune system"), mutations in DNA, transcription errors, [leaky scanning](/wiki/Leaky_scanning "Leaky scanning") by the ribosome causing a [frame shift](/wiki/Frame_shift "Frame shift"), and other causes. Detection of a premature stop codon triggers mRNA degradation by 5' decapping, 3' [poly(A)](/wiki/Poly(A) "Poly(A)") tail removal, or [endonucleolytic cleavage](/wiki/Endonuclease "Endonuclease").[[68]](#cite_note-Isken2007-68)

### Small interfering RNA (siRNA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Small interfering RNA (siRNA)")]

Main article: [siRNA](/wiki/SiRNA "SiRNA")

In [metazoans](/wiki/Metazoan "Metazoan"), [small interfering RNAs](/wiki/Small_interfering_RNA "Small interfering RNA") (siRNAs) processed by [Dicer](/wiki/Dicer "Dicer") are incorporated into a complex known as the [RNA-induced silencing complex](/wiki/RNA-induced_silencing_complex "RNA-induced silencing complex") or RISC. This complex contains an [endonuclease](/wiki/Endonuclease "Endonuclease") that cleaves perfectly complementary messages to which the siRNA binds. The resulting mRNA fragments are then destroyed by [exonucleases](/wiki/Exonuclease "Exonuclease"). siRNA is commonly used in laboratories to block the function of genes in cell culture. It is thought to be part of the innate immune system as a defense against double-stranded RNA viruses.[[69]](#cite_note-Obbard2009-69)

### MicroRNA (miRNA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: MicroRNA (miRNA)")]

Main article: [microRNA](/wiki/MicroRNA "MicroRNA")

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small RNAs that typically are partially complementary to sequences in metazoan messenger RNAs.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) Binding of a miRNA to a message can repress translation of that message and accelerate poly(A) tail removal, thereby hastening mRNA degradation. The mechanism of action of miRNAs is the subject of active research.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[49]](#cite_note-Eulalio2009-49)

### Other decay mechanisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Other decay mechanisms")]

There are other ways by which messages can be degraded, including [non-stop decay](/wiki/Non-stop_decay "Non-stop decay") and silencing by [Piwi-interacting RNA](/wiki/Piwi-interacting_RNA "Piwi-interacting RNA") (piRNA), among others.[[5]](#cite_note-Cooper2000_RNAProcessing-5)[[73]](#cite_note-Siomi2011piRNA-73)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Applications")]

See also: [mRNA vaccine](/wiki/MRNA_vaccine "MRNA vaccine") and [RNA therapeutics](/wiki/RNA_therapeutics "RNA therapeutics")

The administration of a [nucleoside-modified messenger RNA](/wiki/Nucleoside-modified_messenger_RNA "Nucleoside-modified messenger RNA") sequence can cause a cell to make a protein, which in turn could directly treat a disease or could function as a [vaccine](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine"); more indirectly the protein could drive an endogenous [stem cell](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell") to differentiate in a desired way.[[74]](#cite_note-NatRevMat-74)[[75]](#cite_note-GEN-75)

The primary challenges of RNA therapy center on delivering the RNA to the appropriate cells.[[76]](#cite_note-genemed-76) Challenges include the fact that naked RNA sequences naturally degrade after preparation; they may trigger the body's [immune system](/wiki/Immune_system "Immune system") to attack them as an invader; and they are [impermeable](/wiki/Semipermeable_membrane "Semipermeable membrane") to the [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane").[[75]](#cite_note-GEN-75) Once within the cell, they must then leave the cell's transport mechanism to take action within the [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm"), which houses the necessary [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosomes "Ribosomes").[[74]](#cite_note-NatRevMat-74)

Overcoming these challenges, mRNA as a therapeutic was first put forward in 1989 "after the development of a broadly applicable in vitro transfection technique."[[77]](#cite_note-Schlake2012-77) In the 1990s, mRNA vaccines for personalized cancer were developed, typically using unmodified mRNA.[[78]](#cite_note-Pardi2018-78) mRNA based therapies continue to be investigated as a method of treatment or therapy for both cancer as well as auto-immune, metabolic, and respiratory inflammatory diseases.[[79]](#cite_note-Sahin2014-79) Gene editing therapies such as [CRISPR](/wiki/CRISPR_gene_editing "CRISPR gene editing") may also benefit from using mRNA to induce cells to make the desired [Cas](/wiki/Cas9 "Cas9") protein.[[76]](#cite_note-genemed-76)

Since the 2010s, RNA vaccines and other RNA therapeutics have been considered to be "a new class of drugs".[[79]](#cite_note-Sahin2014-79) The first mRNA-based vaccines received restricted authorization and were rolled out across the world during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") by [Pfizer–BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine](/wiki/Pfizer%E2%80%93BioNTech_COVID-19_vaccine "Pfizer–BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine") and [Moderna](/wiki/Moderna_COVID-19_vaccine "Moderna COVID-19 vaccine"), for example.[[80]](#cite_note-pmid35534554-80)
The 2023 [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") was awarded to [Katalin Karikó](/wiki/Katalin_Karik%C3%B3 "Katalin Karikó") and [Drew Weissman](/wiki/Drew_Weissman "Drew Weissman") for the development of effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) New approaches to modulate RNA levels as a therapeutics include the use of antisense oligonucleotides, including for neurodevelopment diseases associated with high mortality.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: History")]

Several molecular biology studies during the early 1950s suggested that RNA played a role in protein synthesis, though the specific role remained unclear.[[8]](#cite_note-Cobb-8) For example, in one of the earliest reports, [Ayaka Stephanie Hogg](/w/index.php?title=Ayaka_Stephanie_Hogg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ayaka Stephanie Hogg (page does not exist)") and his team showed that RNA synthesis was necessary for protein synthesis, specifically during the production of the enzyme [β-galactosidase](/wiki/%CE%92-galactosidase "Β-galactosidase") in the bacterium *[E. coli](/wiki/Escherichia_coli "Escherichia coli")*.[[85]](#cite_note-85) [Arthur Pardee](/wiki/Arthur_Pardee "Arthur Pardee") also found similar RNA accumulation in 1954.[[86]](#cite_note-86) In 1953, [Alfred Hershey](/wiki/Alfred_Hershey "Alfred Hershey"), June Dixon, and [Martha Chase](/wiki/Martha_Chase "Martha Chase") studied *E. coli* infected with bacteriophage T2 and reported that the bacterium's own DNA decreased while the phage's DNA built up inside the infected cells (including DNA containing 5-hydroxymethylcytosine).[[87]](#cite_note-87) In hindsight, this has been discussed as part of the chain of observations that led to the concept of mRNA, and it was not recognized at the time as such.[[8]](#cite_note-Cobb-8)

The idea of mRNA was first conceived by [Sydney Brenner](/wiki/Sydney_Brenner "Sydney Brenner") and [Francis Crick](/wiki/Francis_Crick "Francis Crick") on 15 April 1960 at [King's College, Cambridge](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge"), while [François Jacob](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Jacob "François Jacob") was telling them about a recent experiment conducted by [Arthur Pardee](/wiki/Arthur_Pardee "Arthur Pardee"), himself, and Monod (the so-called PaJaMo experiment, which did not prove mRNA existed but suggested the possibility of its existence). With Crick's encouragement, Brenner and Jacob immediately set out to test this new hypothesis, and they contacted [Matthew Meselson](/wiki/Matthew_Meselson "Matthew Meselson") at the [California Institute of Technology](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology") for assistance. During the summer of 1960, Brenner, Jacob, and Meselson conducted an experiment in Meselson's laboratory at Caltech which was the first to prove the existence of mRNA. That fall, Jacob and Monod coined the name "messenger RNA" and developed the first theoretical framework to explain its function.[[8]](#cite_note-Cobb-8)

In February 1961, [James Watson](/wiki/James_Watson "James Watson") revealed that his [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University")-based research group had been right behind them with a series of experiments whose results pointed in roughly the same direction. Brenner and the others agreed to Watson's request to delay publication of their research findings. As a result, the Brenner and Watson articles were published simultaneously in the same issue of *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")* in May 1961, while that same month, Jacob and Monod published their theoretical framework for mRNA in the *[Journal of Molecular Biology](/wiki/Journal_of_Molecular_Biology "Journal of Molecular Biology")*.[[8]](#cite_note-Cobb-8)
